dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 responding for a female in labor at a residence in the city of Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jamie, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I think I'm going into labor. My water just broke and the contractions are really close together.
Ma'am, can you hear me? What is your name?
Yes, my name is Sarah. Call me Sarah. I barely remember my age, but it's 23.
Which city are we in, Sarah?
Richmond, I think.
What is the date today?
The 29th... June 29th.
Can you tell me what happened today?
I think I'm in labor. I just broke water and contractions started about 10 minutes ago.
Your level of consciousness is Alert and Oriented to Person, Place, Time, and Event.
Your eye-opening response is normal and spontaneous. She's alert and oriented in four domains.
She's in a sits-upright position. Her airway looks patent and maintainable. Any trouble breathing or swallowing, Sarah?
No, I just feel sick.
We're going to take your breathing rate now. Is that okay, Sarah?
Sure.
She's breathing at 22 breaths per minute.
Sarah, do you have asthma or chronic respiratory issues?
Yes, I have mild asthma, but I'm not having a flare-up right now.
Thanks. We'll keep that in mind. Has it been worsening today?
Nope. Things feel okay with my breathing.
Sarah, just to reiterate, do you feel any chest pain or shortness of breath?
No, I don't.
We're going to take your pulse now. Is that okay?
Go ahead.
Pulse is 99 beats per minute and strong.
Sarah, are there any other womb-like symptoms or discomfort with your heart or upper body?
No, just the contractions.
Taking blood sugar. You might feel a slight pinch for a second.
Blood sugar is 110 mg/dL.
Everything looks okay right now, but I'm going to check again shortly.
Will do. We can check it once more for reassurance.
You mentioned being in labor. How severe would you say the pain is? On a scale from 0 to 10.
It's pretty intense, maybe a 10, maybe just below.
Where exactly does the pain start?
It's right here in the lower part. It goes all the way to the backside too.
Does it go anywhere else?
No, just this region.
What does the pain feel like-cramps, pressure?
It's crampy and kind of like pressure.
What seems to make it better or worse?
It gets worse during the contraction. Nothing really helps with it yet.

How long have the contractions been going on? 45. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: I started feeling them an hour ago. My water broke about 10 minutes ago. That's when they began to get closer together.

How long does each contraction last? And how long between them? 47. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: Each one lasts about a minute, and I get them every one and a half minutes or so. 48. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Paramedic: Are you or have you ever been pregnant before? 49. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Patient: I'm 23 years old and in my 39th week of pregnancy. 50. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Partner: That's good information. G2 P0 A1, correct?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Yes.

How are you feeling besides the pain? Any other symptoms like nausea or vomiting? 53. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: I have some nausea and feel really weak. 54. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Paramedic: Any chest pain or shortness of breath? 55. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: No. Just the contractions and the uncomfortable feeling. 56. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Paramedic: How many times have you had any pregnancy complications? 57. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: None.
Do you have any known allergies?
I'm allergic to shellfish, bee stings, and tomatoes.
That's all, right? Any medication allergies?
She's allergic to penicillin as well.

Yeah, I can't take anything with penicillin in it. 63. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Paramedic: We took note of that. Any ongoing medications you take regularly? 64. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Patient: Albuterol for asthma, nothing else.
Sarah, just to confirm-you're 39 weeks pregnant with your second child and this is your first live birth, right?
Yes, that's accurate.

Her skin looks warm, dry, and normal in color. Let me feel her arms and legs for consistent temperature. 68. Secondary Assessment; head and neck exam: Assessment of mental status / Short & Long term memory assessment; Paramedic: She remains oriented and alert throughout. She is Alert and Oriented to Time, Person, Place, and Event.

Hemorrhage; Partner: No sign of active bleeding in the mouth, nose, or behind her ears. 70. Secondary Assessment; thorax/lungs/cardio exam: Breath sounds; Partner: Her breath sounds are loud and equal bilaterally. 71. Secondary Assessment; abdomen exam: Shape and size; Partner: Her abdomen is expanded to full-term size, with a visible fundus. 72. Secondary Assessment; abdomen exam: Tenderness / Rigidity / Guarding; Paramedic: Sarah, may I gently assess the area catching the pain? Is that okay? 73. Secondary Assessment; abdomen exam: Tenderness / Rigidity / Guarding; Patient: Sure, go ahead.
I'll just do a quick check for crowning now. I don't see any signs of delivery.
Blood pressure is 140 over 84.
She's at 100% oxygen saturation.
The ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm with no irregularities right now.
Her blood sugar is 110 at this time, we'll keep checking.
We're going to place a small IV for hydration, okay?
Sure, do what you need to do.
The IV has been placed successfully in her left arm.
Respirations increase to 26 per minute now.
Pulse is 94 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is now 151 over 98.
Oxygen is still on track at 99%.
The EKG is again showing normal sinus rhythm.
Blood sugar drops to zero after the second check.
We're placing the ECG monitor now.
A 4-lead ECG is now on and everything seems stable.
Based on her condition and situation, we are following the childbirth/labor/delivery protocol.
Yes. We'll continue responding within that scope.
Her pulse is now 97 beats per minute.

Respirations are at 28 per minute now. 94. Reassessments; retake vital signs: bp; Partner: Blood pressure has decreased slightly to 138 over 87. 95. Reassessments; retake vital signs: spo2; Partner: Still 99% saturation.
EKG again shows normal rhythm. 97. Reassessments; retake vital signs: glucose; Partner: No change in blood sugar, still at zero.

How would you rate the pain now, Sarah? 99. Reassessments; redo pain assessment: severity; Patient: Same as before, 10. 100. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: signs and symptoms; Paramedic: How are the contractions feeling now? 101. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: signs and symptoms; Patient: Still close, but not much worse.
We'll be transporting to St. Mary's hospital in Richmond.
Sarah, is that your preferred hospital?
Yes, that's good.
We're going to get you safely on the stretcher now. The goal is a safe and comfortable transfer.
She's secured with a 5-point harness for safety.
We've secured you for non-emergent transport. Talk to us if anything feels off.
We turned over care to the staff in the labor and delivery room, including a full report of her condition.
Her GCS is 15 (Eye 4, Verbal 5, Motor 6), meaning she is alert and oriented.
